title: Relate fraction division to fraction multiplication
topic_path: Module 4: Multiplication and division of fractions and decimal fractions
Topic G: Division of fractions and decimal fractions
Relate fraction division to fraction multiplication

assessment_items.question_content:
Sandra wants to use multiplication to solve $\dfrac14 \div 6 = {t}$.

**Which multiplication equation can Sandra use?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenE{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenE{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \greenE{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenE{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$.

The $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenE{6}$.

![Tape diagram showing 1 fourth is equal to 6 equal groups of t](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7046447397f25a927f087d2f802dadfa633b9b94)

The $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$ is ${\maroonD{t}}$.
So, we can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{t}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD6$,  to get the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$, $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{4}}$.

Sandra can use this multiplication equation:


- ${t} \times 6 = \dfrac14$

assessment_items.question_content:
Nell wants to use multiplication to solve ${m} = \dfrac12 \div 8$.

**Which multiplication equation can Nell use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenE{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenE{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \greenE{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenE{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

The $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenE{8}$.

![Tape diagram showing 1 half is equal to 8 equal groups of m](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/63e28017b8753c92109924d1fd2c4f47c234d373)

The $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$ is ${\maroonD{m}}$.
So, we can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{m}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD8$,  to get the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$, $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

Nell  can use this multiplication equation:


- $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{2}} = \greenE{8} \times \maroonD{m}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Emily wants to use multiplication to solve $\dfrac15 \div 3 = {a}$.

**Which multiplication equation can Emily use?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenE{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenE{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \greenE{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenE{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$.

The $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenE{3}$.

![Tape diagram showing 1 fifth is equal to 3 equal groups of a](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4d948bce2af3fcaf2b5c2c56009d1e040ebbf9e6)

The $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$ is ${\maroonD{a}}$.
So, we can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{a}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD3$,  to get the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$, $\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{5}}$.

Emily can use this multiplication equation:

- $\dfrac15=3\times{a}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Hanna wants to use multiplication to solve  $2 \div \dfrac14 = {c}$.

**Which multiplication equation can Hanna use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenE{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenE{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \greenE{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenE{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleD{2}$.


The $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$ is ${\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{4}}}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenE{c}$.
So, we can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac14}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{c}$,  to get the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$, $\purpleD{2}$.

Hanna can use this multiplication equation:

- ${c} \times\dfrac14 = 2$

assessment_items.question_content:
Howard wants to use multiplication to solve  ${w} = 5 \div \dfrac15$.

**Which multiplication equation can Howard use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenE{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenE{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \greenE{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenE{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleD{5}$.


The $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$ is ${\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{5}}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

The $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenE{w}$.
So, we can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{w}$,  to get the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$, $\purpleD{5}$.

Howard can use this multiplication equation:

- $5=\dfrac15\times{w}$

assessment_items.question_content:
Rick wants to use multiplication to solve  ${k} = 7 \div \dfrac13$.

**Which multiplication equation can Rick use?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenE{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenE{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleD{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \greenE{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleD{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenE{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleD{7}$.


The $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$ is ${\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{3}}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]



The $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenE{k}$.
So, we can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac13}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{k}$,  to get the $\purpleD{\text{total}}$, $\purpleD{7}$.

Rick can use this multiplication equation:


- $\dfrac13\times{k} =7$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which equation shows the same relationship as $42=7\div\dfrac{1}{6}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenD{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \greenD{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenD{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleC{7}$.

The $\maroonD{\text{size of each group}}$ is $\maroonD{42}$.

The $\greenD{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenD{\dfrac16}$.
So, we can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{42}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{6}}$,  to get the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC7$.

This number sentence show the same relationship:

- $7=\dfrac16\times42$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which equation shows the same relationship as $72 \times \dfrac18 = 9$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenD{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \greenD{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenD{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleC{9}$.

The $\maroonD{\text{size of each group}}$ is $\maroonD{72}$.

The $\greenD{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenD{\dfrac18}$.

We can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{72}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$,  to get the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC9$.

We can also divide the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC9$, by $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{72}$, or the $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{\dfrac{1}{8}}$.

This number sentence show the same relationship:

- $72=9\div\dfrac{1}{8}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which equation shows the same relationship as $4=\dfrac15\times20$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenD{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \greenD{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenD{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleC{4}$.

The $\maroonD{\text{size of each group}}$ is $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$.

The $\greenD{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenD{20}$.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{20}$,  to get the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC4$.

We can also divide the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC4$, by $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac15}$, or the $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{20}$.

This number sentence show the same relationship:

- $4\div20=\dfrac15$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which equation shows the same relationship as $\dfrac14 \div 2 = \dfrac{1}{8}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenD{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \greenD{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenD{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleC{\dfrac{1}{4}}$.

The $\maroonD{\text{size of each group}}$ is $\maroonD{\dfrac18}$.

The $\greenD{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenD{2}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac18}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{2}$,  to get the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC{\dfrac14}$.

We can also divide the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC{\dfrac14}$, by $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac18}$, or the $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{2}$.

This number sentence show the same relationship:

- $\dfrac1{4}=2\times\dfrac1{8}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which equation shows the same relationship as $\dfrac12 = 3\times\dfrac{1}{6}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenD{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \greenD{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenD{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleC{\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

The $\maroonD{\text{size of each group}}$ is $\maroonD{\dfrac16}$.

The $\greenD{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenD{3}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac16}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{3}$,  to get the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC{\dfrac12}$.

We can also divide the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC{\dfrac12}$, by $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac16}$, or the $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{3}$.

This number sentence show the same relationship:

- $\dfrac1{2}\div3=\dfrac1{6}$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which equation shows the same relationship as $2= 4\times\dfrac{1}{2}$?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Multiplication and division are related:

$\begin{align}
\greenD{\text{number of groups}} \times \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \times \greenD{\text{number of groups}} = \purpleC{\text{total}} \\\\
\\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \greenD{\text{number of groups}} =\maroonD{\text{size of groups}} \\\\
\purpleC{\text{total}} \div \maroonD{\text{size of groups}} =\greenD{\text{number of groups}}
\end{align}$

In this fact family, the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$ is $\purpleC{2}$.

The $\maroonD{\text{size of each group}}$ is $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$.

The $\greenD{\text{number of the groups}}$ is $\greenD{4}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply the $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$, and $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{4}$,  to get the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC{2}$.

We can also divide the $\purpleC{\text{total}}$, $\purpleC{2}$, by $\maroonD{\text{size of the groups}}$, $\maroonD{\dfrac{1}{2}}$, or the $\greenE{\text{number of the groups}}$, $\greenD{4}$.

This number sentence show the same relationship:

- $2\div4=\dfrac1{2}$
